cards.text: When an exception is thrown, any allocated {{c1::resources}} (memory, file handles, etc.) in the current function will {{c2::leak}} unless they are managed by objects with {{c3::destructors}} (RAII).

cards.question: What's wrong with this code if processData() throws?
cards.answer: Memory leak! If processData() throws, delete[] ptr never executes and memory is leaked.
cards.explanation: When an exception is thrown, the function exits immediately. Without RAII, the cleanup code (delete[]) never runs.

cards.question: Why doesn't this code leak if processData() throws?
cards.answer: No leak because unique_ptr's destructor runs during stack unwinding and automatically deletes the memory.
cards.explanation: RAII ensures cleanup even when exceptions are thrown. The unique_ptr destructor releases the memory automatically.

cards.text: Exception handling has {{c1::minimal}} overhead when no exception is thrown, but throwing and catching exceptions is {{c2::expensive}} due to stack unwinding and object destruction.

cards.front: Should exceptions be used for normal control flow?
cards.back: No, exceptions should only be used for exceptional circumstances, not normal control flow. They are expensive and make code harder to follow.

cards.text: Destructors should {{c1::not throw}} exceptions. If a destructor throws during stack unwinding while another exception is active, {{c2::std::terminate()}} is called.

cards.question: What happens when this code runs?
cards.explanation: throw 2 starts unwinding. During unwinding, ~Bad() throws. Two active exceptions cause std::terminate().

cards.front: Why were dynamic exception specifications (throw(...)) deprecated in C++11?
cards.back: They were error-prone, not enforced at compile time, caused runtime overhead, and didn't work well with templates. They were replaced with noexcept.

cards.text: The {{c1::noexcept}} specifier indicates a function won't throw exceptions. If a noexcept function does throw, {{c2::std::terminate()}} is called immediately.

cards.explanation:
To write exception-safe code:
  • Never use raw pointers with new/delete
  • Use RAII classes (unique_ptr, vector, fstream, etc.)
  • Ensure destructors clean up resources
  • Destructors must not throw
  • Acquire resources in constructors
  • Release resources in destructors

cards.front: When should you avoid using exceptions due to performance concerns?
cards.back: In performance-critical code where exceptions might be thrown frequently, or in embedded/real-time systems where predictable performance is required.

cards.text: The basic exception safety guarantee ensures no {{c1::resource leaks}} and objects remain in a {{c2::valid state}}, while the strong guarantee ensures either {{c3::complete success}} or {{c4::no effect}} (rollback).
